region_of_origin: Chaoshan (潮汕), Guangdong Province, China; widely adopted in Singapore
food_subgroup: Steamed taro paste dessert with coconut cream and ginkgo nuts

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Teochew orh nee / yam paste dessert. Closest parent concept is taro-based dessert preparation. FoodOn URI left null pending formal ontology submission.
ontology.foodex2_description: Teochew steamed taro paste dessert with coconut cream and ginkgo nuts
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0DSW is the closest FoodEx2 parent for taro/yam-based dessert preparations; no exact code exists for orh nee specifically.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of orh nee is approximately 200g, comprising steamed mashed taro with lard or coconut oil and sugar, topped with approximately 10g ginkgo nuts and a 30ml coconut cream drizzle, yielding approximately 436 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Orh Nee — Component calculation: HPB Singapore taro base + USDA FoodData Central coconut cream and ginkgo nuts
nutrient_source.organization: Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Food Composition Database; USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g values derived by component calculation: taro nutritional base from Singapore HPB Foodo database; coconut cream and ginkgo nut values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Weighted blend calculated for the standard 200g bowl composition (taro ~160g, ginkgo nuts ~10g, coconut cream ~30ml). Values are consistent with the input record marked as verified confidence 1.0.

gi_source.title: Taro GI reference — University of Sydney GI Database; fat-modulated estimate for orh nee preparation
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (Glycemic Index Foundation)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Taro GI of approximately 54–56 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database. The significant fat content from lard or coconut oil (~10.8g fat per 100g) moderately lowers the effective GI of the dish to approximately 52. Glycemic load per 200g serving calculated as: (GI 52 × available carbohydrate 54g per serving) / 100 = 28.1. This is classified as HIGH glycemic load per serving despite low GI, due to the large carbohydrate quantity per bowl.

hypertension.guidance: Orh nee is one of the few high-calorie Singaporean desserts genuinely low in sodium (~45mg/100g; ~90mg per bowl), making it acceptable for hypertensive patients from a sodium standpoint, though the high saturated fat content from lard or coconut oil warrants moderation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (GL ~28 per 200g bowl) due to taro starch and added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should treat orh nee as an occasional indulgence only: GL ~28 per bowl is equivalent to approximately 1.6 bowls of white rice; a half-portion (~100g, GL ~14) with the coconut cream drizzle omitted substantially reduces glycaemic impact, and a fruit-based dessert is preferred for routine consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~12g per 200g bowl from lard or coconut oil) and moderate caloric density (436 kcal/bowl)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high saturated fat content (~6g/100g; ~12g per bowl) from lard or coconut oil raises LDL cholesterol risk; patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to approximately 100g and prefer a coconut-oil-based version over lard, while noting the very low sodium content is a positive feature.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat and high added sugar contribute to systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: High saturated fat (~12g/bowl from lard or coconut oil) and high sugar (~24g/bowl) may promote systemic inflammation and AGE formation relevant to RA; occasional consumption in small portions is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Orh nee (芋泥) is a classic Teochew Chinese dessert widely served in Singapore at weddings, Chinese New Year banquets, and traditional Teochew restaurants. It consists of steamed taro (yam) that is mashed to a smooth, silky paste with lard or coconut oil and sugar, then topped with whole cooked ginkgo nuts (~10g) and a drizzle of rich coconut cream (~30ml). The texture is dense, smooth, and intensely fragrant, with a naturally purple-grey colour from the taro. A standard bowl is approximately 200g and delivers approximately 436 kcal, 4.4g protein, 21.6g fat (~12g saturated fat from lard or coconut oil), 56g carbohydrate (~24g sugar including added sugar), and 5g dietary fibre. Sodium is very low at approximately 90mg per bowl — unusually low for a Singaporean dessert. GI is approximately 52 (low), but the glycemic load per bowl is approximately 28 (high), making it a high-GL dessert despite the moderate GI. GINKGO NUT SAFETY NOTE: raw ginkgo nuts contain 4-methoxypyridoxine and are toxic; the cooked dose of 8–10 nuts used in orh nee is considered safe for healthy adults. Clinically, orh nee is high concern for Type 2 Diabetes (GL 28/bowl), moderate concern for cardiovascular disease (high SFA), and low concern for hypertension (very low sodium). Source: Singapore HPB Foodo (taro base) + USDA FoodData Central (coconut cream, ginkgo nuts); component calculation. Confidence: HIGH.

confidence_reason: Per-100g values derived from component calculation using Singapore HPB Foodo (taro base) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (coconut cream, ginkgo nuts), both approved primary sources. Input record was marked verified with confidence 1.0. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database taro reference (GI 54–56) with fat-modulation adjustment, clearly documented. All core macros are non-zero and nutritionally consistent with the dish composition.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the orh nee component blend (taro + lard/coconut oil + ginkgo nuts) is not available as a single entry in Singapore HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04; individual ingredient MUFA/PUFA values exist but a reliable weighted blend for this specific preparation was not calculable with sufficient confidence. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the assembled dish is not reported in the HPB or USDA source entries used. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for coconut oil and taro; lard contributes negligible trans fat). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — this is a plant-based dessert with no animal-derived vitamin B12 source; lard contributes negligible B12). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — taro, coconut cream, and ginkgo nuts contain no meaningful vitamin D). Dietary flag note: is_vegan set to false because traditional preparation uses lard; coconut-oil versions would be vegan. Halal status applies to coconut-oil-based preparations; lard-based versions are not halal. GI glycemic load per serving calculated as (52 × 54g available carbohydrate per 200g serving) / 100 = 28.1. GINKGO NUT SAFETY: raw ginkgo nuts are toxic due to 4-methoxypyridoxine; the cooked quantity (~10g / 8–10 nuts) used in standard orh nee is within the safe consumption range for healthy adults.
